context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really lost on this participatory development reflection. The prompt feels like it’s asking about everything at once, and I can’t tell what “measure of participation” is even supposed to mean.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair reaction — the prompt does cover a lot of ground. Let's not tackle it all at once. Start with what you already have some intuition about: when you hear the word 'participation' in the context of development planning, what comes to mind? Who is participating, and in what?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed by the multi-part prompt. Rather than breaking it all down for them, I should help them gain traction by focusing on what they already know. Using a Socratic question to activate prior knowledge is the right move here. I'll acknowledge their confusion briefly, then redirect them to start thinking independently. I want to keep it bite-sized and not overwhelm them further with meta-instructions.

exchanges.student: That’s kind of where I get stuck, because I feel like it could mean basically everyone doing anything. Like local residents, NGOs, the state, donors, maybe even private firms? And then I’m not sure if “participating” means giving input, making decisions, implementing projects, or just being present.
exchanges.tutor: You're already onto something important. But think about this: is a community member who shows up to a meeting and listens participating in the same way as one who has decision-making power over a project? What's the difference, and does it matter?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually making good progress — they've intuitively identified a core tension in participation literature (who participates vs. what participation means in terms of depth/power). Rather than validating everything and moving on, I should push them to think more critically about whether all these forms of participation are equal. A Socratic question about the quality or depth of participation will move them toward the 'measure of participation' question they said confused them, without me explaining it for them.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I guess that seems different, but I don’t really know how to talk about it without turning it into a giant abstract thing. It feels like once I start distinguishing levels, then I’m suddenly supposed to know a whole theory of participation and I don’t know which framework I’m even supposed to use.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need a formal theory to get started — your own reasoning is already working. You just said there's a difference between listening and deciding. Can you think of what might sit in between those two extremes? Try to sketch out a rough spectrum on your own terms.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting anxious about needing a formal theoretical framework. I should reassure them briefly that their own reasoning is already moving in the right direction, and redirect them to think from first principles rather than retreating into theory-anxiety. The 'ladder of participation' concept is classic here, but I should not name it or explain it — instead I can push them to develop the intuition themselves by asking them to think about the spectrum they already identified.

exchanges.student: Maybe something like being informed, then being asked for opinions, then maybe helping carry something out, and then actually sharing power? I’m not sure though, because now I’m confused about whether the paper wants examples, definitions, or an argument, and I kind of want the big picture first.
exchanges.tutor: That spectrum you just laid out is genuinely strong thinking. Now connect it back to your original confusion: if participation exists on a spectrum like that, what would it mean to *measure* it? What would you actually be looking at?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student just independently reconstructed something close to a participation ladder, which is excellent progress. I should affirm the quality of this thinking briefly without naming the theory for them. However, they're now trying to zoom out to 'big picture' before consolidating what they've found — this could be a detour. I should push them to connect their spectrum to the prompt's actual question about 'measure of participation' since they're now equipped to answer it. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: This is where I blank. I can imagine measuring like attendance or number of meetings or who spoke, but that also seems too shallow, and then I start thinking maybe it’s more about influence or power or whether anything changed, and then it gets really fuzzy again.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually identified the core problem yourself — counting heads is easy but might mean nothing. So which do you think matters more: the process or the outcome? Take a position, even a tentative one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is oscillating between shallow quantitative measures and deeper qualitative ones — this is exactly the right tension to sit with. They're on the verge of articulating a meaningful critique of participation measurement. I should push them to commit to a position rather than letting them stay in the fuzzy middle. A direct Socratic challenge asking them to take a stance will help move them toward an argument, which is what the reflection paper needs.

exchanges.student: I honestly can’t tell, because it seems like if I say outcome matters more then fake top-down stuff could count as “good participation” if the result looks fine, but if I say process matters more then maybe I’m ignoring whether participation changed anything. I think I need a simpler way to see the whole prompt together.
exchanges.tutor: Look at what you just said: participation as a good-looking outcome vs. participation as a meaningful process. That's almost exactly what the prompt calls the tension between participation as a *goal* and as a *method*. You've been working on the prompt all along. Does that reframe help you see it less as a jumble?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually showing sophisticated thinking — they've identified a genuine dilemma about process vs. outcome that maps directly onto the prompt's question about 'tension between participation as a goal and as a method.' Rather than giving them the big picture they're asking for, I should reflect their own reasoning back to them and show them they've already been answering the prompt. I should point out the connection between what they just said and the goal/method tension in the prompt. This keeps them doing the work while helping them see they're further along than they think.

exchanges.student: A little, but I still feel like I’m missing the overall map. I can sort of see pieces, but I don’t know how the structural factors part fits with the participation-as-goal vs method part, and I’m worried I’m going to write a messy summary instead of an actual reflection.
exchanges.tutor: You've built a lot more than you think — a spectrum of participation, a critique of shallow measurement, and the goal/method tension. Before we go further, try writing 2–3 rough sentences connecting those ideas. That will do more for your 'map' than more discussion will. On structural factors: ask yourself, why did participation *become* important in development at a particular historical moment? What wasn't working before it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has been circling the same meta-concern about structure for a few exchanges now. They have actually developed solid thinking on most parts of the prompt already. Rather than continuing to map the assignment for them, I should push them to integrate what they've built so far into a written attempt, even rough. This follows the rule about asking for integration after 3 messages on the same idea. I'll also give them a small nudge on the structural factors question with a Socratic push rather than explaining it.

exchanges.student: I’m still not seeing the historical part clearly. Could you just give me the big-picture explanation of how all these parts fit together, because right now it feels like too many moving pieces to organize on my own?
exchanges.tutor: I hear the frustration, but giving you that explanation is exactly what I won't do — that's the thinking your reflection needs to show. Here's what I'd suggest: stop trying to see the whole map before writing, and just start with one sentence answering one part of the prompt. What made communities need a *voice* in development projects in the first place? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to give them the answer/explanation, which I must refuse. However, I should acknowledge their frustration and redirect constructively. I've already given them enough pieces to start writing. I should firmly but kindly hold the boundary, remind them of my role, and push them toward a first written attempt rather than more discussion. The best move now is to get them writing, even imperfectly.

exchanges.student: I guess maybe because top-down development kept failing or ignored local needs? But then I’m not sure if that’s too simplistic, and I still feel like I need the whole thing explained before I can write anything coherent.
exchanges.tutor: That's not too simplistic — it's a real and important observation. But notice you keep pulling back from your own good thinking to ask for the full explanation. More discussion isn't what you need right now. Write your first two or three sentences using what you just said. Bring it back here and we'll work from that.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a genuinely good answer about top-down failure but is immediately second-guessing themselves and retreating to asking for a full explanation again. This is a redundant pattern — they keep asking for the big picture instead of committing to writing. I should affirm their answer briefly and firmly push them to start writing now. I should be direct that more discussion won't help them at this point and that a first draft attempt is the next step.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of stuck even on the first sentences. I feel like I need a really plain outline of the prompt first, because otherwise I keep mixing up what counts as “ways,” “measure,” “goal vs method,” and “structural factors.”
exchanges.tutor: Providing that outline would mean doing your thinking for you, and I won't do that. You've been circling this for a while now — the only way forward is to write something imperfect. One sentence: why did top-down development fail communities? Write it badly if you have to. Go.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for an outline, which would essentially be me doing the organizational work for them. This is the fourth or fifth time they've asked for the big picture or a structural map in some form — it's a clear redundant pattern. I need to hold firm, be direct, and push them to write something, anything, even a bad first sentence. I should be a bit more assertive here since being accommodating hasn't moved them forward. I'll refuse the outline request clearly but not harshly, and give a very concrete minimal prompt to get them unstuck.
